s: Greek Revival temples built for worn-out sailors on Staten Island
w: Captain Robert Richard Randall left his estate in 1801 to build a home for 'aged, decrepit and worn-out sailors.' The result: an 83-acre campus of Greek Revival temples on Staten Island's North Shore — one of the finest ensembles in America. The sailors moved out in 1976. Now a cultural campus and botanical garden.
_facts: Founded under the will of Captain Robert Richard Randall (died 1801), who left his Manhattan estate to create a sailors' retirement home.
Five Greek Revival buildings along the central mall, built from the 1830s, are among the finest examples of the style in the US.
Operated as Sailors' Snug Harbor retirement home until 1976, when the remaining residents were moved to New Jersey.
83-acre campus on the Kill Van Kull; managed since 1976 by Snug Harbor Cultural Center and Botanical Garden.
Designated a National Historic Landmark district; several individual structures are New York City landmarks.

s: Victorian photographer, queer life, a cottage above the Narrows
w: Alice Austen picked up a camera at ten in 1876 and spent fifty years documenting street life with directness. She lived openly with partner Gertrude Tate in this Gothic cottage above the Narrows. Bankrupt at 84, she entered the poorhouse; Life magazine published her photos and funded a nursing home. She died in 1952.
_facts: Alice Austen (1866-1952) began photographing at age ten and produced some 8,000 glass-plate negatives.
Lived at Clear Comfort, 2 Hylan Boulevard, Rosebank, Staten Island for most of her life.
Austen lived openly with Gertrude Tate, her companion of 30 years.
Lost her savings in the 1929 crash; forced to sell in 1945 and entered a poorhouse at age 79.
Life magazine published her photographs in 1951, raising money for a nursing home where she died in 1952.

s: Manhattan's last old-growth forest, with Lenape caves in the bedrock
w: On a schist ridge 200 feet above the Hudson, 196 acres have never been cleared. The Wecquaesgeek called it Shorakapkok — 'the sitting place' — and sheltered in caves here for nearly 700 years. The caves are still in the rock. The oldest trees on the island. Walk the ridge trail and find silence two miles from Midtown.
_facts: Contains the largest remaining old-growth forest on Manhattan island; 196 acres in Inwood.
Known to the Wecquaesgeek as Shorakapkok ('the sitting place'); inhabited for nearly 700 years before European contact.
A glacially scoured schist ridge rises 200 feet above the Hudson River.
Natural Lenape caves in the bedrock are still visible on the park's trails.
The legend of Peter Minuit's 1626 purchase of Manhattan is associated with this site.

s: The Quaker who defied Stuyvesant and won religious freedom in 1662
w: John Bowne built this house in 1661 and held Quaker meetings in defiance of Director-General Stuyvesant's ban. Stuyvesant had him shipped to Holland; the West India Company overruled it. The 1657 Flushing Remonstrance — signed by 30 residents — is a forerunner of the First Amendment. Still standing on Bowne Street.
_facts: Built 1661 by John Bowne (1627-1695) at 37-01 Bowne Street, Flushing, Queens.
Bowne held Quaker meetings defying Director-General Peter Stuyvesant's ban on Quaker worship.
Stuyvesant had Bowne arrested in 1662 and sent to Holland; the Dutch West India Company overruled Stuyvesant.
The Flushing Remonstrance of 1657, signed by 30 residents, is considered a precursor to the First Amendment.
Designated a National Historic Landmark; open for tours.

s: 1758 Bronx fieldstone farmhouse — looted by Hessians, now a museum
w: Isaac Valentine built this fieldstone house in 1758; the British seized it and Hessian troops wintered here. The Varian family — one became the 63rd Mayor of New York — held it until 1905. Moved to Bainbridge Avenue in 1965, restored as the Museum of Bronx History. The stone walls Valentine laid.
_facts: Built 1758 by Isaac Valentine; the second oldest house and oldest surviving farmhouse in the Bronx.
Occupied by British and Hessian forces during the Revolutionary War.
Owned by the Varian family; Isaac Varian served as the 63rd Mayor of New York City.
Sold in 1905; moved to 3266 Bainbridge Avenue in 1965 and restored 1965-68.
Now the Museum of Bronx History; operated by the Bronx County Historical Society.

s: Sorolla's Spain in a forgotten Beaux Arts campus above 155th Street
w: Archer Milton Huntington built Audubon Terrace from 1904 on Audubon's former farm. The Hispanic Society holds El Greco, Goya, Velazquez, plus Joaquin Sorolla's 14-panel 'Vision of Spain' and 15,000 books printed before 1701. One of New York's great secrets. Almost nobody goes.
_facts: Founded 1904 by Archer Milton Huntington on the former estate of naturalist John James Audubon.
Holds one of the most important collections of Spanish and Portuguese art outside the Iberian Peninsula.
Library contains over 15,000 books printed before 1701 and thousands of manuscripts.
Joaquin Sorolla's 14-panel 'Vision of Spain' (1912-19) is the centrepiece of the main hall.
Part of the Audubon Terrace Historic District, a National Register site with eight Beaux Arts buildings on one block.

s: Maryland soldiers died here holding off the British so Washington could flee
w: On August 27, 1776, 400 Maryland soldiers crossed enemy lines three times to hold this farmhouse while Washington's army escaped. About 256 died. Washington reportedly said: 'Good God, what brave fellows I must this day lose.' The Vechte-Cortelyou house was reconstructed in 1935 in J. J. Byrne Playground.
_facts: Built 1699; original demolished after the Civil War; reconstructed on original foundations in 1935.
Scene of the Battle of Brooklyn, August 27, 1776 — the largest battle of the Revolutionary War.
400 Maryland soldiers ('the Maryland 400') held the house against the British to allow Washington's retreat.
Approximately 256 of the 400 Maryland defenders died in the action.
Designated a New York City landmark; now a museum in J. J. Byrne Playground, Park Slope.

s: Oldest school in New York State — 1787 wooden building still stands inside
w: Founded 1786, the first school chartered by the New York State Regents. The original 1787 wooden schoolhouse survives in a courtyard, swallowed by the 1905 Gothic Revival building around it. Streisand, Neil Diamond and Bernard Baruch all attended. A landmark inside a still-operating high school.
_facts: Founded 1786 as Erasmus Hall Academy, the first secondary school chartered by the New York State Regents.
The original 1787 clapboard schoolhouse survives in the inner courtyard at 899-925 Flatbush Avenue.
Built on land donated by the Flatbush Reformed Dutch Church; turned over to the public school system in 1896.
Named for Desiderius Erasmus, the Dutch Renaissance humanist.
Notable alumni include Barbra Streisand, Neil Diamond and Bernard Baruch.

s: Italian ices churned from scratch since 1944 — sixty flavours, no dairy
w: Nicola Benfaremo started making Italian ices in his garage in 1944, a block from Louis Armstrong's house. Three generations later the family still churns small batches daily, achieving a creamy texture without any dairy. Sixty rotating flavours: lemon, tamarind, champagne. Open seasonally. The queue moves slow.
_facts: Founded 1944 by Nicola Benfaremo in a garage in Corona, Queens; shop at 52-02 108th Street.
All ices made in-house in small batches; creamy texture through churning without dairy.
Approximately 60 flavours rotate seasonally including lemon, tamarind, chocolate and champagne.
Located one block from the Louis Armstrong House Museum at 34-56 107th Street.
Featured in the opening credits of the CBS sitcom The King of Queens.

s: A Founding Father who opposed slavery in 1785 — his manor in Jamaica, Queens
w: Rufus King signed the Constitution in 1787 and introduced a bill to prohibit slavery in western territories — among the first such attempts. He was the last Federalist presidential nominee, losing to Monroe in 1816. His Jamaica estate, circa 1730, is a National Historic Landmark in an 11.5-acre park.
_facts: Built circa 1730 at 150th Street and Jamaica Avenue, Jamaica, Queens; expanded in 1755 and the 1800s.
Home of Rufus King (1755-1827), signer of the US Constitution in 1787.
King introduced a bill in 1785 to prohibit slavery in western territories.
Last Federalist presidential nominee; lost to James Monroe in 1816.
Designated a National Historic Landmark; set within Rufus King Park, an 11.5-acre public park.

s: Oldest house in the Bronx — Washington slept here, twice
w: Frederick Van Cortlandt completed this Georgian rubblestone house in 1749. Washington used it before the Battle of White Plains in 1776, and again during the final march on New York in 1783. One of the most intact Georgian interiors in the city. Museum since 1897.
_facts: Built 1748-49 by Frederick Van Cortlandt; oldest surviving house in the Bronx.
Georgian rubblestone facade; located in the southwestern corner of Van Cortlandt Park.
Washington used it as headquarters before the Battle of White Plains (1776) and during the march on New York (1783).
The Van Cortlandt family occupied it for 140 years before it became a museum in 1897.
Designated a New York City landmark and National Historic Landmark.

s: A million New Yorkers in numbered trenches here — open for visits
w: Since 1869 the city has buried its unclaimed dead here — an estimated million people in numbered wooden boxes, three deep in long trenches. HIV patients, stillborns, the unnamed. Before that: a Civil War prison camp, yellow fever quarantine, a drug rehab centre. Ferries run from City Island.
_facts: New York City's potter's field since 1869; estimated one million people buried here.
Burials conducted by incarcerated individuals from Rikers; wooden boxes stacked three deep.
Prior uses: Civil War prisoner camp (1865), yellow fever quarantine, drug rehabilitation centre and women's asylum.
Approximately 1 mile long; located east of City Island in Long Island Sound.
Transferred from the Department of Correction to Parks in 2019; public ferry visits from City Island began 2021.

s: A mural-lined boardwalk on the most polluted waterway in New York
w: Newtown Creek carries 30 million gallons of spilled petroleum from the Greenpoint oil spill — one of the largest in US history — plus 150 years of industrial runoff. EPA Superfund site since 2010. An 1,800-foot nature walk along Paidge Avenue has murals and native plantings. Great blue herons fish here.
_facts: Newtown Creek: 3.5-mile tributary of the East River forming the Brooklyn-Queens border.
Estimated 30 million gallons of petroleum beneath Greenpoint from Mobil and others — discovered in the 1970s.
Declared an EPA Superfund site in 2010.
The Newtown Creek Nature Walk along Paidge Avenue is approximately 1,800 feet with murals and native plantings.
Great blue herons and other waterbirds fish the creek regularly despite severe pollution.

s: Eight 140-foot sewage eggs in polished steel, glowing blue over Brooklyn
w: Eight anaerobic digester tanks — each 140 feet tall, clad in corrugated stainless steel — dominate the Newtown Creek Wastewater Treatment Plant, lit blue at night. They convert sewage sludge to methane that powers the facility. The plant processes 310 million gallons a day. Monthly tours. Visible from the BQE.
_facts: Newtown Creek Wastewater Treatment Plant at 329 Greenpoint Avenue; NYC's largest sewage facility.
Eight 'digester eggs' — anaerobic digesters — each 140 feet tall; completed 1998.
Clad in corrugated stainless steel; illuminated blue at night; visible from roads in Brooklyn, Queens and Manhattan.
Each egg holds approximately 3 million gallons of sludge; methane produced powers the plant.
Free public tours run monthly.

s: A Belt Parkway beach where horseshoe crabs swarm ashore each May
w: Tucked behind the Belt Parkway east of Sheepshead Bay, this Gateway National Recreation Area beach hosts one of the largest urban spawnings of Atlantic horseshoe crabs — unchanged for 450 million years — each May and June. Accessible through an unmarked parkway gap. Shorebirds follow for the eggs.
_facts: Part of the Gateway National Recreation Area; accessible via a gap in the Belt Parkway.
Atlantic horseshoe crabs (Limulus polyphemus) stage a major urban spawning event each May-June.
Horseshoe crabs are chelicerates, related to spiders rather than crustaceans; unchanged for 450 million years.
The beach is important for migrating red knots and other shorebirds that eat horseshoe crab eggs.
Located on the north shore of Rockaway Inlet between Sheepshead Bay and Gerritsen Beach.

s: Where Dylan went electric and the Beatles played Queens, 1964-65
w: Built 1923 for the US National Championships. The Beatles played 16,000 fans here in August 1964. Bob Dylan's first electric shows — preceding Newport — happened on this grass in August 1965. Sinatra, Hendrix, The Who, Simon and Garfunkel all followed. The Tudor horseshoe still stands.
_facts: Built 1923 for the West Side Tennis Club at Burns Street, Forest Hills, Queens; capacity approximately 14,000.
Hosted the US National Championships (precursor to the US Open) until 1977.
The Beatles performed here August 28-29, 1964 during their first US tour.
Bob Dylan played his first electric sets here August 28, 1965, preceding the Newport Folk Festival controversy.
Frank Sinatra, Jimi Hendrix, The Who, Simon and Garfunkel and Diana Ross all performed here.

s: Cold War Nike missile silos under the dunes, now rewilded with deer
w: Fort Tilden guarded New York Harbor from 1917 through the Cold War, with Nike Ajax batteries capable of nuclear warheads. The Army left in 1974; the National Park Service let the site rewild. Battery Harris gun emplacements stand on dunes above the Atlantic. Trails pass concrete ruins and pitch pine. The deer watch.
_facts: Established 1917 to defend New York Harbor; named for former Governor Samuel J. Tilden.
Housed Nike Ajax missile batteries capable of nuclear warheads; operational mid-1950s to 1974.
Army vacated 1974; transferred to National Park Service as part of Gateway National Recreation Area.
Battery Harris, with two 16-inch gun emplacements, stands on a dune above the Atlantic.
Large portions have rewilded; white-tailed deer are commonly seen among overgrown officer housing.
